title: ‘Weapons’ Is Why We Go to the Movies, With Zach Cregger!
format: Sean Fennessey flies solo for the last Big Picture before the fall-festival break. Brian Raftery drops in to tease his summer series on the Bush-era 2000s. Then Sean pins Michael Shanks's body-horror 'Together' against Zach Cregger's 'Weapons,' walks through a long syllabus of the films behind it, and closes with a spoiler-heavy sit-down with Cregger himself.

segments.heading: Brian Raftery on his summer series

films.title: Unbreakable
films.note: Raftery's white whale was M. Night Shyamalan, the guest he wanted and couldn't land; he calls this 'a really interesting movie about 2000.'

films.title: Signs
films.note: The Shyamalan title he tags as 'a really amazing kind of actual 9/11 movie.'

films.title: The Happening
films.note: The third Shyamalan he couldn't choose between: 'an amazing movie for a gazillion reasons.'

films.title: Eddington
films.note: The movie Raftery can't stop thinking about from his heavy summer at the theater.

films.title: 28 Years Later
films.note: His most fun moviegoing experience of the year: he lost his mind, alone, at the Burbank AMC over the gang ending.

films.title: Amadeus
films.note: Went to a 70mm screening for his wife's first time; the projector broke and they finished on Tubi. 'Perfect movie, no notes.'

films.title: The Naked Gun
films.note: The reboot he's most excited for; he's seen the 1988 original 'ten million times' and just wants comedies back on the big screen.

films.title: Together
films.note: Written and directed by Michael Shanks. Dave Franco and Alison Brie (a real-life couple) play Tim and Millie, a relationship at a crossroads who fall into a cursed sunken church, drink the water, and start physically fusing. A modern riff on codependency body horror that premiered noisily at Sundance and Neon bought big. It's already about $13M at the box office. Sean finds the setup preposterous and the metaphor hammered flat (the muscle-relaxer shower gag, the sex-stall fusion), but the leads are good and the movie is a fine time. It ends with the two literally merged into one being while their parents meet at lunch.

segments.heading: ‘Weapons’
segments.note: Sean's favorite thing at the movies

films.title: Weapons
films.note: Zach Cregger's second feature after 'Barbarian,' pitched by Cregger to the trades as 'hereditary meets Magnolia.' Josh Brolin, Julia Garner, Alden Ehrenreich, Austin Abrams, Cary Christopher, Benedict Wong and Amy Madigan; shot by Larkin Seiple. Seventeen kids sprint out of Justine Gandy's class one night and the town's grief curdles into paranoia, told across seven chapter-structured POVs. Sean's favorite of the year: the structure, the performances (Madigan as the witch Aunt Gladys, Christopher as the lone kid left behind), the constantly-moving camera, grief themes left as subtext, and a gory, euphoric final 15 minutes. Warner Bros. won the script in a bidding war Jordan Peele was reportedly furious to lose.

segments.heading: The ‘Weapons’ syllabus
segments.note: Sean's influence list for the movie

films.title: Village of the Damned
films.note: The British original about blonde children who emit power; Sean notes Carpenter's lesser 1995 remake. The eeriness of what's really going on with little kids.

films.title: Rosemary's Baby
films.note: Ruth Gordon's Minnie Castevet, look and scheme, is the template Sean sees in Amy Madigan's Gladys.

films.title: The Blood on Satan's Claw
films.note: Piers Haggard's British folk horror about children changed by exposure to the devil's remains; Sean doubts Cregger had it in mind but calls it a good fit.

films.title: The Stepford Wives
films.note: Cited for its body-and-mind control of a whole community.

films.title: Invasion of the Body Snatchers
films.note: The Philip Kaufman version, a prevailing force overtaking consciousness and flesh, though Cregger says Body Snatchers movies aren't big for him.

films.title: Ordinary People
films.note: Redford's Best Picture winner about a suburban family unable to cope with a son's death. Sean's strongest emotional comparison; Cregger admits he's only seen it once.

films.title: The Witches of Eastwick
films.note: George Miller; witches casting spells and controlling minds and bodies.

films.title: They Live
films.note: Carpenter: a society that isn't as it seems.

films.title: The 'Burbs
films.note: Joe Dante's what's-in-that-house comedy-horror; a favorite of Sean's wife.

films.title: Short Cuts
films.note: Altman's Raymond Carver adaptation, the ensemble-convergence DNA behind Magnolia.

films.title: The Sweet Hereafter
films.note: Egoyan's Russell Banks adaptation about a town that loses a number of children to an accident and can't cope.

films.title: Magnolia
films.note: The interconnected structure and needle drops; Ehrenreich's mustachioed cop mirrors John C. Reilly, Garner mirrors Melora Walters. Cregger confirms the cold open is a direct homage and that he fed the trades the 'hereditary meets Magnolia' line.

films.title: Little Children
films.note: Todd Field's Tom Perrotta adaptation about suburban insecurity and quiet desperation.

films.title: Insidious
films.note: James Wan; the closest standard-horror visual palette and the kids-in-a-nether-region idea.

films.title: Prisoners
films.note: Villeneuve and Deakins's child-abduction grief-mystery, an obvious influence; Cregger even scouted its Atlanta neighborhood.

films.title: The Shrouds
films.note: Cronenberg's grief film Sean pairs with 'Weapons' as a double feature about death and awful decision-making.

films.title: Barbarian
films.note: Cregger's debut, now streaming on Netflix; different but connected, and yes, basements again.

films.title: Son of Saul
films.note: The hyper-subjective, perched-on-the-shoulder camera rule Cregger and DP Larkin Seiple set for every chapter.

films.title: Needful Things
films.note: The TV trailer that 'seared something' into Cregger: a movie he imagined that was better than the one he saw. Sean flagged it from Cregger's own bidding-war episode.

films.title: Field of Dreams
films.note: Amy Madigan 'side A': ebullient, disarming warmth.

films.title: Gone Baby Gone
films.note: Amy Madigan 'side B': the lethal, reptilian core Cregger needed for Gladys.

films.title: Raising Arizona
films.note: The 'full Point Break' spirit behind reshooting the finale into a multi-location kid chase, lawn-mower cutaway and all: four extra days he had to spring on his line producer.

films.title: Jaws
films.note: Prompted by the 50th-anniversary doc. Cregger relates to Spielberg's traumatic shoot after his own near-collapse (six-day weeks, double shifts) while making 'Weapons.'

TV / docs: The Monsters Are Due on Maple Street (Twilight Zone)

The Lord of the Rings.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
